---
date: "2026-06-22"
---

## The Standard Error Is the Whole Ballgame

**Learning objectives.** By the end you can:

- Explain why the standard error decides credibility
- Make one $\hat\beta$ tell two opposite verdicts
- Identify the shape of $\boldsymbol\Omega$ in your data
- Report SE flavor, clustering level, and cluster count

::: notes
Welcome to Week Two, Mentor Session two, part one of six. Last week we asked when a number is a finding. This week you learned the machinery that turns a regression into a claim — O L S in matrix form, the Gauss-Markov guarantees, the Frisch-Waugh-Lovell trick, and what happens to your standard error when the errors misbehave. Today we put the two weeks together and stare at one uncomfortable fact. In empirical finance, computing the point estimate beta hat is usually the easy part. This part frames the whole session, then meets the example that should unsettle you a little.
:::

## The Easy Part and the Hard Part

- Your laptop hands you $\hat\beta$ in milliseconds
- Under Gauss–Markov, $\hat\beta$ is **unbiased** regardless of error correlation
- Credibility lives almost entirely in the **standard error**
- We argue today — we do not lecture

::: notes
Start with the asymmetry at the heart of today. Computing the point estimate, beta hat, is the easy part. Your laptop will hand you beta hat in milliseconds, and under Gauss-Markov it is unbiased no matter how the errors are correlated. So the coefficient is essentially free, and it is honest. The credibility of your work — whether a referee believes you, whether the finding survives — lives almost entirely in the standard error. That is the claim we will defend. Bring your written answers to the three warm-ups, because we are going to argue, not lecture. By the end you should believe that the standard error is not a small technical afterthought; it is the result.
:::

## Same $\hat\beta$, Two Different Verdicts

- One regression, one dataset, one estimate $\hat\beta = 0.42$
- Walk away with "significant at 1%" **or** "can't reject zero"
- What decides? A choice about $\boldsymbol\Omega$, a matrix you never see
- $\boldsymbol\Omega$ = variance–covariance matrix of the **errors**
- Same data, different story about $\boldsymbol\Omega$, different conclusion

::: notes
Here is the fact that should unsettle you. You can run one regression, on one dataset, get one point estimate — beta hat equals zero point four two — and walk away with either a triumphant "significant at one percent" or a deflating "can't reject zero." And the thing that decides which, the entire difference between publish and not-yet, is a modeling choice you make about a matrix you never see. That matrix is capital Omega, the variance-covariance matrix of the errors. The choice is how you estimate the middle of the variance sandwich: X-prime-X inverse, times X-prime, times Omega, times X, times X-prime-X inverse. Same beta hat. Same data. A different story about Omega gives you a different t-statistic and a different conclusion. That is what we mean when we say the standard error is the whole ballgame.
:::

## Maya's Fair-Lending Result

- Loan rates on a ZIP-tier indicator; controls; $N = 9{,}000$ loans
- Classical: $\hat\beta_1 = 0.42$, standard error $0.07$, $t = 6.0$
- Mentor's question: are those 9,000 loans truly independent?
- Loans cluster by **lender** and by **state**
- Clustered: same $\hat\beta_1 = 0.42$, standard error $0.19$, $t = 2.2$

::: notes
Recall Maya's fair-lending result from Chapter two-point-four. She regressed loan interest rates on a ZIP-code-tier indicator, controlling for credit score, loan size, and income, across nine thousand loans. The coefficient came back at zero point four two percentage points, with a classical standard error of zero point zero seven, for a t-statistic of six point zero — a number screaming through a megaphone. Then her mentor asked the only question that mattered: are those nine thousand loans really nine thousand independent pieces of evidence? They are not. Loans cluster by lender, through shared pricing models, and by state, through shared regulation and local economies. Once Maya let the errors inside a lender be correlated — that is clustering — the same beta hat, zero point four two, came back with a standard error of zero point one nine and a t-statistic of two point two. The estimate never moved a hair. The zero point zero seven was the lie; the zero point four two was right the whole time.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
One regression can yield **two opposite verdicts** with the same $\hat\beta = 0.42$ — the difference is your story about $\boldsymbol\Omega$, the error variance–covariance matrix. The coefficient was never the problem; the standard error is the whole ballgame.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part one: the same point estimate, beta hat equals zero point four two, gave Maya either a t of six point zero or a t of two point two — significant or not — and the only thing that changed was her story about Omega, the variance-covariance matrix of the errors. The coefficient was right the whole time; the standard error decided the verdict. In part two we ask why this happens mechanically, and which direction the naive standard error gets it wrong.
:::
